title: Elder Moor
text:
Elder Moor clings to the edge of the Kreigstan Wilds, a settlement born from necessity and stubbornness. Founded generations ago by loggers seeking the valuable timber of the ancient forest, it has grown into a proper village with a temple, tavern, and small marketplace. The locals are a hardy folk, pragmatic and suspicious of outsiders until trust is earned.

The village takes its name from the moor that stretches to the south, a boggy expanse that marks the transition between forest and swamp. On foggy mornings, the mist rises from the moor and mingles with woodsmoke from the settlement's chimneys, creating an atmosphere of perpetual twilight that newcomers find unsettling but residents have long since grown accustomed to.

Elder Moor serves as the starting point for many adventurers, offering basic supplies and information about the surrounding regions. The Temple of Three Gods provides blessings for travelers, and the local bounty board always has work for those willing to risk the dangers of the wilds. The innkeeper knows everyone's business, and the blacksmith can repair a blade or forge new arrows. It is not much, but it is civilization's last outpost before the wilderness consumes everything.

title: Dalhurst
text:
Dalhurst is the jewel of the western coast, a city of stone walls and busy harbors where trade routes from across the known world converge. Ships flying the flags of a dozen nations anchor in Dalhurst Bay, their holds full of spices, silks, and treasures that find their way to the city's legendary markets. Merchants grow fat on the profits, nobles scheme for advantage, and the common folk scrape by as best they can.

The city is divided into distinct districts. The harbor district never sleeps, with dockhands loading and unloading cargo at all hours while sailors crowd the waterfront taverns. The merchant quarter buzzes with commerce, its shops and stalls offering everything from exotic fruits to enchanted trinkets. The old town climbs the hill above the harbor, its narrow streets home to artisans, temples, and the grand houses of the noble families who trace their lineages back to the city's founding.

Beneath this prosperous surface, darker currents flow. The Thieves Guild operates from the shadows, their hands in every pocket and their ears in every room. Smugglers use hidden coves to avoid the harbor taxes. And whispers persist of a cult that meets in the catacombs beneath the old cemetery, worshipping something that predates the Three Gods themselves.

title: Larton
text:
Larton sits at the crossroads between Dalhurst and the southern territories, a market town that has grown prosperous from its position along the main trade route. Farmers from the surrounding countryside bring their produce here, merchants stop to rest and resupply, and the local crafters have built a reputation for quality goods at fair prices.

The town lacks Dalhurst's grandeur but possesses its own rough charm. The central square hosts a weekly market that draws visitors from miles around, and the town's three taverns compete fiercely for their patronage. The temple is modest but well-maintained, and the town guard takes its duties seriously, knowing that bandits would love nothing more than to plunder the merchant caravans that pass through.

Larton's position between the coast and the swamps has given its people a particular perspective on the world. They know that danger lies in every direction, that the wilderness is always pressing against civilization's boundaries, and that only constant vigilance keeps the darkness at bay. Newcomers often find the locals' wariness off-putting until they spend a night listening to the wolves howl in the distance.

title: Aberdeen
text:
Aberdeen rises from the edge of the Southern Swamps like a defiance of nature itself, built on ground that should not support construction by people too stubborn to live anywhere sensible. The town is a maze of boardwalks and stilt-houses, connected by rope bridges that sway in the wind and creak ominously underfoot. Visitors often develop a deep appreciation for solid ground.

The people of Aberdeen have adapted to their environment in ways that outsiders find peculiar. They harvest bog iron from the swamp and forge it into blades of unusual quality. They cultivate strains of rice that grow in the shallows and brew a potent whiskey from swamp grain that burns like fire going down. Their healers know the medicinal properties of plants that grow nowhere else, and their fishermen bring in catches that would seem fantastical anywhere but here.

Living beside the swamp has its costs. The drowned dead sometimes wander close to town, forcing the militia to mount patrols along the perimeter. Diseases unique to the marsh claim lives every year. And the oldest residents speak of bargains struck with things in the deep water, agreements that must be honored lest something far worse than fever or undead come calling.

title: East Hollow
text:
East Hollow takes its name from the natural depression in which it was built, a bowl-shaped valley that provides shelter from the winds that sweep across the southern regions. The town began as a waystation for travelers heading toward Whaeler's Canyon and has grown into a community of several hundred souls who make their living from the east-west trade.

The hollow's geography provides natural defenses that the founders reinforced with wooden palisades and watchtowers. Goblin raids from the canyon have taught the residents to be vigilant, and every adult knows how to handle a weapon. The militia drills regularly, and the town maintains a small arsenal of crossbows and spears for distribution when the warning bells sound.

Despite the danger, East Hollow has developed a reputation as a place where unusual goods change hands. Hunters bring in monster parts for sale to alchemists and enchanters. Prospectors sell ore samples from the canyon's mineral deposits. And occasionally, someone arrives with an artifact recovered from the ruins that dot the region, seeking buyers with deep pockets and few questions.

title: Whalers Abyss
text:
Whalers Abyss perches on the eastern edge of the canyon that shares its name, a town built into the cliff face itself. Buildings cling to ledges and nestle into natural caves, connected by stairs carved from the living rock and bridges that span dizzying drops. Visitors with a fear of heights generally do not stay long.

The town exists primarily to support expeditions into the mountains and the elven lands beyond. Guides who know the safe paths through goblin territory command premium prices, and the local merchants stock supplies specifically chosen for extended wilderness travel. The blacksmith specializes in climbing equipment, and the healer has extensive experience treating injuries from falls and goblin arrows.

Whalers Abyss maintains a careful neutrality with the goblin clans, paying tribute to avoid raids and occasionally hiring goblin scouts when their knowledge of the canyon proves useful. This pragmatic arrangement disgusts some visitors but has kept the town intact for decades while more militant settlements were destroyed. The residents shrug at criticism and point out that they are still alive to be criticized.

title: Rotherhine
text:
Rotherhine marks the end of the Mountain Pass and the beginning of the eastern lands, a fortress city built to guard the route between the two halves of the Human Empire. Its walls have withstood goblin hordes, troll attacks, and at least one dragon, standing as a testament to dwarven engineering and human determination.

The city is divided between the garrison and the civilian population, with the military maintaining strict control over the fortifications and the approaches to the pass. Soldiers patrol constantly, and the armories are always stocked for siege. The civilians who live here have accepted that their home is first and foremost a military installation, their needs secondary to the strategic importance of the position.

Despite its martial character, Rotherhine has developed a vibrant commercial district where merchants trade goods from both sides of the mountains. Dwarven crafts from Kazer-Dun fetch high prices, and eastern luxuries attract wealthy buyers from the western settlements. The city's taverns cater to soldiers, merchants, and adventurers alike, offering everything from cheap ale to exotic wines smuggled through the pass.

title: Falkenhafen
text:
Falkenhafen is the heart of the Human Empire, a city of soaring spires and crowded streets where half a million souls live, work, and scheme beneath the shadow of the Imperial Palace. Here the great decisions are made, the laws proclaimed, and the fate of nations determined by those who hold power or seek to claim it.

The city sprawls across both banks of the Falken River, connected by twelve bridges of varying antiquity and splendor. The Imperial District surrounds the palace, home to the nobility and those who serve them. The temple district hosts the grandest cathedrals of the Church of Three Gods. The merchant quarter rivals Dalhurst for commercial activity. And the warrens, the tangled slums where the poor struggle to survive, spread through the spaces that polite society prefers to ignore.

Unknown to most, the Empire's ruler is not the true King but an imposter, a witch who has somehow replaced him and now works toward unknown ends. The Keepers, a secret organization, work to expose this deception and rescue the real King from wherever he has been imprisoned. But the witch has her own agents, and the shadows of Falkenhafen hide dangers that most citizens cannot imagine.

title: Elven Outpost
text:
The Elven Outpost is not truly a settlement in the human sense but a waystation where the elves have chosen to interact with the outside world. A handful of graceful buildings rise among ancient trees, their architecture seeming to grow from the forest rather than having been constructed within it. Human visitors often find the place unsettling, beautiful but subtly wrong in ways they cannot articulate.

The elves who staff the outpost belong to the diplomatic faction that favors cooperation with humans. They trade elven goods, craft and enchanted items of surpassing quality, for human manufactures and news of the outside world. They also serve as intermediaries for those seeking to enter the elven lands proper, though such permission is rarely granted and never given lightly.

Relations between the outpost and human visitors are cordial but distant. The elves are polite, even friendly by their standards, but they never forget that humans are mayflies who will be dead in a few decades while they continue for centuries more. This perspective colors every interaction, creating a gulf of understanding that goodwill alone cannot bridge.
